votes.reason: The Python code iteratively computes the exact same sum as the Coq recursive function, applying the same modulo conditions and arithmetic operations.
The Python code implements the same logic as the Coq code, using an iterative approach instead of recursion to compute the sum of modified elements based on their index.
The Python code correctly implements the logic of the Coq fixpoint using a loop, matching the modulo conditions, index updates, and arithmetic operations exactly.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The sum_squares_aux function iterates through the list with an index i, applying the same conditional logic: if i mod 3 == 0, use x*x; else if i mod 4 == 0, use x*x*x; else use x. The index increments after each element, matching the Coq recursive call with (S i). The sum_squares_spec checks if ans equals the result starting from index 0, matching the Coq definition.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. Both iterate through the list with an index starting at 0, incrementing by 1 each step. At each position, both check if i mod 3 == 0 (square the element), else if i mod 4 == 0 (cube the element), else use the element directly. The sum is accumulated identically. The spec function checks equality of ans with the computed sum starting at index 0.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code. It iterates through the list, starting with index 0, and for each element: if index mod 3 == 0, adds x*x; else if index mod 4 == 0, adds x*x*x; else adds x. The index increments after each element. This matches the Coq recursive definition exactly, and sum_squares_spec checks if ans equals the computed sum starting from index 0.

Both compute the same per-index rule (i%3==0 -> x^2, elif i%4==0 -> x^3, else x), iterate from i=0 across the list, and sum with arbitrary-precision integers; the spec checks equality to sum_squares_aux(0, lst) in both.

Python mirrors the Coq recursion and indexing, using the same modulo conditions and arithmetic on big integers; for i starting at 0 and incrementing per element, it yields the same sum, and the spec matches.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: iterates from i=0, applies the same priority of conditions (i%3==0 then i%4==0 else x), and sums squares/cubes/raw values. Python ints and modulo match Coq Z and Nat.modulo for nonnegative i.
